svg.text: Selection converts candidate variation into a new output distribution
340 rounds from 74 OLMo / Qwen runs; next-spread tests use 258 consecutive transitions with leave-one-run-out fits
Three distances live on one candidate-score axis
Kept minus whole pool measures the selector.
Kept minus the model's own pool measures the update.
training displacement = pool-supply shift + selector gap · outside candidates create the pool-supply shift, so p ≠ q; in self-only pools p = q and the two coincide
q own generated mean p whole offered pool mean k kept training-target mean v separate behavioral value
The self-relative gap tracks behavioral movement best — and its edge grows under pool mixing
Pearson r of each distance with the round-to-round change in the behavioral value; behavioral pull bridges
to that separate readout, not a rival selector.
The chain: displacement moves the generator's mean, and the mean sets next-round spread
Δ own mean = 0.005 + 0.827 × training displacement (all 258, r = 0.864; mixed 72: 0.015 + 0.791×, r = 0.913)
q moves toward 0 or 1 → less next-round spread q moves toward 0.5 → more next-round spread (round number is not a term)
Held out one whole run at a time, the chain beats
leave-one-run-out R² predicting the model's own next-round candidate spread;
every held-out run is excluded from the fit that predicts it.
Fully factorized before selection (pool-supply shift + 0.96·agreement·spread): leave-one-run-out R² 0.684 vs 0.540 overall (225
Leaving out entire conditions gives the same ordering: 0.788 vs 0.652 overall, 0.619 vs 0.255 mixed.
Source: experiments/spread_conversion_model.json (scripts/analysis_spread_conversion_model.py). Panel A positions are schematic; panel B
uses logged rounds, panels C/D use consecutive-round transitions.
